content.body: Biochemistry is the chemistry of biological molecules. The four major classes are carbohydrates, proteins, lipids, and nucleic acids.

CARBOHYDRATES: Contain C, H, O in ratio (CH₂O)n.
Monosaccharides: glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆), fructose, galactose
Disaccharides: sucrose (glucose+fructose), maltose, lactose
Polysaccharides: starch, glycogen, cellulose (all = glucose polymers)
Test: Benedict's test — reducing sugars turn brick-red on heating.

PROTEINS: Polymers of amino acids linked by peptide bonds.
20 amino acids. Each has: amino group (−NH₂), carboxyl group (−COOH), R group (variable).
Primary structure: sequence of amino acids
Secondary: α-helix or β-pleated sheet (H-bonds)
Tertiary: 3D fold (R group interactions)
Quaternary: multiple polypeptide chains
Test: Biuret test — purple colour with proteins.

Peptide bond formation (condensation):
Amino acid 1 + Amino acid 2 → Dipeptide + H₂O

Hydrolysis (digestion): Protein + H₂O → Amino acids

questions.question: The test for reducing sugars is:
questions.explanation: Benedict's reagent (blue) + reducing sugar → heated → brick red/orange precipitate forms.

questions.question: Starch, glycogen and cellulose are all polymers of:
questions.explanation: All three polysaccharides are made of glucose monomers, differing only in linkage type and branching.

questions.question: Amino acids are linked together by:
questions.explanation: Peptide bond: −CO−NH− formed by condensation between carboxyl and amino groups of adjacent amino acids.

questions.question: The Biuret test detects:
questions.explanation: Biuret reagent (NaOH + CuSO₄) turns purple/violet in the presence of peptide bonds (proteins).

questions.question: Sucrose is a disaccharide composed of:
questions.explanation: Sucrose (table sugar) = glucose + fructose joined by a glycosidic bond.

questions.question: Protein denaturation involves:
questions.explanation: Denaturation = unfolding of 3D structure. Primary sequence (peptide bonds) intact but shape changes → protein no longer functional.

questions.question: Glycogen is the storage carbohydrate in:
questions.explanation: Glycogen = animal starch. Stored in liver (blood glucose regulation) and muscles (energy reserve).

questions.question: Number of water molecules released forming a dipeptide from two amino acids =
questions.explanation: Condensation reaction: one H₂O released per peptide bond formed. Dipeptide = 1 bond = 1 H₂O.

questions.question: The R group in an amino acid determines:
options: Whether it is an amino acid
questions.explanation: The R group (side chain) is different for each of the 20 amino acids — it determines solubility, charge, shape.

questions.question: Iodine solution tests for:
questions.explanation: Iodine + starch → blue-black colour. The iodine molecules fit inside the starch helix structure.

questions.question: Cellulose differs from starch/glycogen because:
options: Has β-glucose linkages making it indigestible by most animals
questions.answer: Has β-glucose linkages making it indigestible by most animals
questions.explanation: Cellulose uses β-1,4 glycosidic bonds → straight chains → structural (cell walls). Humans lack cellulase enzyme.

questions.question: Quaternary protein structure refers to:
questions.explanation: Haemoglobin has quaternary structure: 4 polypeptide chains (2α + 2β) combined together.

questions.question: What elements are found in proteins but NOT in carbohydrates?
questions.explanation: Amino acids contain N (and some contain S, e.g. cysteine). Carbohydrates contain only C, H, O.

questions.question: Hydrolysis of a protein is the reverse of:
questions.explanation: Peptide bond formation is condensation (−H₂O). Hydrolysis adds water to break peptide bonds back to amino acids.

questions.question: Enzymes are proteins. Their activity depends on:
questions.explanation: The active site's specific shape enables substrate binding. Denaturation changes this shape → enzyme no longer works.

questions.question: Reducing sugars include:
questions.explanation: Reducing sugars have free aldehyde/ketone groups. Sucrose and polysaccharides are non-reducing.

questions.question: The molecular formula of glucose is:
questions.explanation: Glucose (and fructose): C₆H₁₂O₆ — a hexose sugar. (C₁₂H₂₂O₁₁ is sucrose/maltose — disaccharides).

questions.question: α-helix and β-pleated sheet are examples of protein:
questions.explanation: Secondary structure is the local regular folding of the polypeptide chain, maintained by hydrogen bonds.

questions.question: Sucrose is a non-reducing sugar because:
options: Both anomeric carbons (C1) are involved in the glycosidic bond
questions.answer: Both anomeric carbons (C1) are involved in the glycosidic bond
questions.explanation: The bond between glucose C1 and fructose C2 blocks both reducing ends — no free aldehyde/ketone groups remain.

questions.question: In human digestion, proteins are broken down to:
questions.explanation: Proteases (pepsin, trypsin, chymotrypsin) hydrolyse proteins to amino acids, which are absorbed in the small intestine.
